evidence: Both files stay within the `contracts` package and encode protocol identifiers/metadata without importing runtime subsystems, keeping boundary direction stable.

`protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` each expose package-level contract IDs (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `APIConnectionError`) created at init time via pure string composition helpers, with no I/O or mutable shared state.
Contract catalog assembly is split across file-local `...CatalogSpecs()` functions, reducing direct coupling to callers but leaving aggregation behavior implicit outside these files.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Contract registration/aggregation ownership is implicit rather than enforced by a single explicit module boundary, which can allow future drift in how contracts are introduced.

evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines a reusable constructor path (`protocolActionContract`) for contract entries, while `startram_contracts.go` constructs entries inline, creating two parallel declaration styles in the same package.

Both files encode the same conceptual artifact (contract ID + descriptor + metadata) but with different construction conventions, which weakens ownership clarity for how new contract families should be added.
Package-level exported contract IDs are declared as mutable `var` values in both files even though they appear semantically constant, blurring contract identity intent.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` both still import `io/ioutil` while the same files also use modern `os`/`io` APIs, keeping a legacy package dependency alive in core runtime paths.
Both modules are long-lived service paths (import lifecycle and log streaming), so keeping deprecated package usage duplicated across them increases maintenance friction and API inconsistency.

`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/main.go`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` each build separate runtime bags (many function fields), then run local resolve/merge/validate layers before calling concrete logic.

Runtime seam construction is repeated rather than centralized: `default*Runtime`, `resolve*Runtime`, and validator functions reappear with slightly different conventions, so cross-module handoffs are not predictable to readers.

Package-level shared runtimes (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`) add implicit integration state outside explicit constructors.

Boilerplate guard/resolve flows are duplicated in multiple call paths (`goseg/broadcast/transitions.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, `goseg/main.go`), creating repetitive internals with small behavioral differences.

`ApplyBroadcastTransition` and `ApplyBroadcastMutation` in `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go` duplicate almost the same state-read/update/notify sequence, increasing local branching and maintenance surface for every behavior change.

Runtime wrappers in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` repeatedly resolve runtime before each operation (`runTransitionLifecycle`, `emitWorkflowTransition`, `publishTransition`, `dispatchUploadImport`), which adds local control-flow noise.
goseg/shipworkflow/startram.go contains only package declaration plus generic explanatory comments about module splitting, with no executable contract or API documentation.

goseg/system/system.go mirrors the same template-like pattern (package declaration + high-level split rationale), suggesting repeated boilerplate text islands rather than concise package docs in canonical doc files.

goseg/startram/doc.go and goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go contain broad one-line package summaries, while the extra narrative comments in startram.go/system.go duplicate organizational intent instead of adding actionable technical context.
goseg/shipworkflow/startram.go appears to be a leftover anchor file after decomposition ('split into focused service runtime files') but retains the legacy topical filename without active code.
goseg/system/system.go similarly retains a former central module name while now acting as a placeholder note, indicating migration cleanup was not completed to stable package-doc conventions.

The same package-level intent is split across placeholder files and doc.go files in neighboring modules (startram/uploadsvc), indicating partial transition to canonical package documentation patterns.
holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large flat directories for the reviewed areas: goseg/shipworkflow/ has 45 files, goseg/system/ has 43 files, goseg/config/ has 41 files.

goseg/system/ already has purpose-specific subdirectories (maintenance/, metrics/, storage/, wifi/service/, wifi/transport/) but reviewed files goseg/system/apt.go and goseg/system/broadcast_info.go remain in the root package, indicating inconsistent placement boundaries.

goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go contains generic lifecycle/polling helpers while the same directory also contains multiple domains (newship, startram, urbit_operations, workflow), making ownership and discovery dependent on filename scanning.
goseg/config/bootstrap.go and goseg/config/config.go combine bootstrap, runtime context, global mutable config state, and startram cache state in one package-level namespace.
README.md defines explicit backend quality-gate commands and a path-aware `Runtime Contract Gate` with targeted contract tests for protocol and StarTram surfaces.
README.md still depends on manual execution for core commands (`go test ./...` and integration-tag tests), so local/CI parity depends on process discipline rather than one enforced command path.

Contract declaration files (protocol_contracts.go, startram_contracts.go) contain critical identifiers/messages but no in-file guardrails (such as compile-time invariants) to complement external test enforcement.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Quality gates are present but partly process-driven; contract safety relies more on external workflow discipline than directly enforced invariants near declaration sites.

evidence: Wi-Fi API symbols mix `WiFi` and `Wifi` in the same surface (`WiFiRuntimeService`, `IsWifiEnabled`, `ListWifiSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi`) in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go.
Lower-level runtime keeps yet another mixed style (`newWiFiRuntime`, `parseNmcliWifiDevices`, `ErrWifiInterfaceNotFound`) in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go.
Broadcast transition and mutation paths in goseg/broadcast/transitions.go use direct guard clauses and straightforward state update flow.

Docker subsystem event loop and health loop in goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go and subsystem_health.go are generally linear, but repeated nil-context fallback branches add avoidable flow noise.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Repeated defensive `ctx == nil` fallbacks across long-running loops make lifecycle behavior harder to reason about.

evidence: Generic helpers in goseg/config/config_update_registry.go keep many patch handlers typed, but parsing still routes through `interface{}` entry points.
Wi-Fi runtime seam validation in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go enforces callback presence, reducing nil-call runtime failure risk.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Config patch parsing remains runtime-typed at API boundaries, so incorrect shapes are detected late.

evidence: README mandates typed API usage including `ActionToken` (README.md:99), but contracts expose `ActionVerb` as the action token type (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:33-34).
Protocol contract definitions use `ActionVerb` consistently (goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go:9-11), reinforcing that README guidance is out of sync with the runtime contract API.
Config runtime context is populated at package init time (`init` sets `setRuntimeContext(defaultRuntimeContext())`) in goseg/config/config.go:194-195.

Startup then mutates runtime context again (`setDebugMode`, `initializePaths`) in goseg/config/bootstrap.go:53 and :103, making effective runtime context depend on both import-time and startup-time ordering.
Acronym casing diverges across C2C APIs: `C2CConnectAction` in contracts vs `C2cCheckWith`, `RunC2cCheckFn`, `StartC2cCheck` in goseg/main.go and goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go.
Wi-Fi naming also drifts between `WiFi` and `Wifi` forms in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go and goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go.

Long-running APIs in subsystem and routines silently convert nil contexts to `context.Background()` (`startDockerHealthLoop`, `dockerListenerWithRuntime`, `dockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime`, `Check502Loop`, `StartBackupRoutinesWithContext`) across goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go, goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go, and goseg/routines/system/system.go.

Other APIs reject missing runtime/context prerequisites explicitly (e.g. `ApplyBroadcastTransition` errors when runtime is missing in goseg/broadcast/transitions.go), creating inconsistent caller expectations for boundary contracts.
Upload session opening validates token with a nil request (`ValidateUploadSessionTokenFn(..., nil)`) in goseg/importer/importer.go:340.

Upload request validation later passes the real request (`ValidateUploadSessionTokenFn(..., r)`) in goseg/importer/importer.go:645, so auth behavior can differ between session creation and upload execution paths.

The open-endpoint action is a first-class protocol contract (`ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`) in goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go, so this divergence affects a defined external auth boundary.

findings.summary: The contracts package uses inconsistent construction patterns for equivalent catalog entries, which weakens architectural coherence.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` routes entries through `protocolActionContract(...)` and namespace/action helpers.
`startram_contracts.go` builds `contractCatalogEntry` inline with hard-coded structure and does not reuse the same entry-construction path.
Both files publish package-level contract IDs as `var`, mixing mutable-looking identifiers with immutable contract semantics.
findings.suggestion: Standardize one declaration pipeline for all contract families (e.g., a shared builder/registry helper), and make contract IDs immutable by construction where possible; this should be applied across both files so new contract families follow one architectural convention.

findings.summary: Deprecated `io/ioutil` remains in active runtime modules, duplicating legacy dependency usage.
evidence: `goseg/importer/importer.go` imports `io/ioutil` alongside modern `os`/`io` usage.
`goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` also imports and uses `io/ioutil` (`ioutil.ReadDir`) in a production loop.
The same codebase already relies on `os.ReadFile`/`os.WriteFile` patterns in other assigned files (for example orchestration runtime/file seams), so dependency style is split.
findings.suggestion: Replace remaining `io/ioutil` calls with `os`/`io` equivalents (`os.ReadDir`, `os.ReadFile`, `os.WriteFile`) in both modules, then remove `io/ioutil` imports to standardize dependency usage.

findings.summary: Cross-module handoffs depend on repeated runtime-bag patterns instead of a single seam model.
evidence: Each module defines its own large function-field runtime structs and local resolver/validator mechanics (`startupInitRuntime`, `importerRuntime`, `bootstrapRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, orchestration runtime ops).
The same lifecycle (default -> optional override -> validation -> invoke) is rebuilt per package, so boundary translation logic is scattered and inconsistent.
This pattern forces callers to understand package-specific seam plumbing before understanding the business operation itself.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam composition convention: one small constructor-injected dependency struct per subsystem entrypoint, with a common validation helper and explicit narrow interfaces only at true package boundaries.

findings.summary: Repeated runtime resolution and guard scaffolding obscures local intent in core flow functions.
evidence: `ApplyBroadcastTransition` and `ApplyBroadcastMutation` duplicate near-identical guard, state copy, update, and notify flow.
`WiFiRuntimeService` methods repeatedly call `prepareForUse` then perform small operation-specific logic; the same guard sequence appears across many methods.

Workflow and bootstrap paths repeatedly wrap operations with resolve/validate checks (`resolveWorkflowRuntime`, runtime method wrappers, `bootstrapRuntime` wrapper methods), adding local indirection in otherwise straightforward operations.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared operation templates (for example `withResolvedRuntime`, `withBroadcastMutation`, `withPreparedWiFiRuntime`) so each method only expresses domain behavior, not repeated guard/resolution mechanics.

findings.summary: Legacy-named placeholder files remain after decomposition, signaling an unfinished migration to canonical package documentation layout.
evidence: startram.go and system.go contain only package declarations and comments describing prior splitting/refactor state.
Parallel packages already use doc.go for package commentary, showing mixed old/new documentation placement patterns.
No runtime/API definitions in the placeholder files indicate these are migration remnants rather than intentional module entrypoints.
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration by moving any necessary package comments into canonical doc.go files per package, then remove or repurpose placeholder files so filenames map to active behavior rather than historical context.

findings.summary: Repeated high-level explanatory comments form boilerplate debt that restates structure without defining concrete contracts.
evidence: Comments in startram.go and system.go are generic process statements ('split into focused modules') rather than package semantics, constraints, or usage.
The comment style is near-template prose and duplicated intent across files.
Given no code in those files, the comments become the only content and still do not provide operationally useful documentation.
findings.suggestion: Replace generic boilerplate with concise package contracts in one canonical location per package (doc.go), focusing on responsibilities, invariants, and external touchpoints; remove duplicated narrative comments.

findings.summary: File-level elegance is reduced by inconsistent documentation placement and zero-behavior files with broad narrative comments.
evidence: Two files have no executable declarations beyond package statement, increasing navigation noise.
Package commentary is split between doc.go and non-doc topic files, forcing extra file hops for equivalent information.
Narrative comments are not tightly scoped to file-level behavior because the files have no behavior.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on doc.go for package-level docs and keep non-doc files behavior-bearing; if a package needs no extra docs, prefer removing narrative-only stubs entirely.

findings.summary: shipworkflow is an overloaded flat package that mixes multiple domain slices without folder-level boundaries.
evidence: directory_profiles reports goseg/shipworkflow/ with 45 files (23 production, 22 test), exceeding the mixed-concern threshold.

The same directory contains unrelated concern groups (newship*, startram*, urbit_operations*, workflow*), while lifecycle.go adds generic polling/config-persist helpers rather than a clear domain aggregate.
lifecycle_test.go is a placeholder smoke test, which reinforces that placement/import wiring is being tested more than cohesive behavior ownership.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: create `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle/` and move `lifecycle.go` + `lifecycle_test.go` there, keeping exported surface minimal. Stage 2: create domain folders `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/startram/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/urbitops/` and move files by prefix group in that order (newship -> startram -> urbit_operations). Stage 3: add a thin `goseg/shipworkflow/api.go` facade for stable callers. Stage 4: update imports with `rg -n "groundseg/shipworkflow" goseg` then `goimports -w goseg/shipworkflow/**/*.go goseg/**/*.go`, and validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/handler/...`.

findings.summary: system package shows partial reorganization with key files still stranded at root, creating ambiguous module boundaries.
evidence: directory_profiles shows goseg/system/ has 43 files while dedicated subdirectories already exist (maintenance/, metrics/, storage/, wifi/service/, wifi/transport/).
apt.go provides maintenance/update orchestration but remains in root instead of maintenance-aligned placement.
broadcast_info.go aggregates runtime metrics and disk/wifi/system snapshots for broadcast use but remains in root even with a metrics subpackage present.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: move `apt.go`/`apt_test.go` to `goseg/system/maintenance/apt.go` and keep compatibility via temporary forwarding wrappers in `goseg/system/`. Stage 2: move `broadcast_info.go` to `goseg/system/metrics/broadcast_info.go` (or `goseg/broadcast/collectors/system_info.go` if ownership is broadcast-facing). Stage 3: replace root exports with explicit package-level facades and deprecate them in one release cycle. Stage 4: update imports using `rg -n "groundseg/system" goseg` and `goimports -w goseg/system/**/*.go goseg/broadcast/**/*.go goseg/handler/**/*.go`, then validate with `go test ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/broadcast/... ./goseg/handler/...`.

findings.summary: config package combines bootstrap orchestration, runtime process context, and mutable service state in one top-level ownership boundary.
evidence: directory_profiles reports goseg/config/ with 41 files and mixed responsibilities (bootstrap, update schema/registry, storage/minio/wireguard/version, runtime context).
bootstrap.go performs initialization orchestration, filesystem seeding, persistence, and auth/session setup in one package-level flow.
config.go holds runtime context defaults + mutable startram/global config state, increasing coupling between process context and persistent config concerns.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: introduce `goseg/config/runtimectx/` and move `RuntimeContext`, setters/getters, and default resolvers from `config.go`. Stage 2: introduce `goseg/config/bootstrap/` for initialization workflow from `bootstrap.go`; keep `config.Initialize` as facade. Stage 3: isolate mutable startram/global state into `goseg/config/state/` and keep persistence/update logic in existing config core. Stage 4: run import updates via `rg -n "groundseg/config" goseg` and `goimports -w goseg/config/**/*.go goseg/**/*.go`; validate with `go test ./goseg/config/... ./goseg/startuporchestrator/... ./goseg/main...`.

findings.summary: Canonical contract IDs are exported as mutable vars, weakening boundary guarantees.
evidence: `UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`, `C2CConnectAction`, and `APIConnectionError` are package-level `var` exports, not immutable constants/accessors.
README marks protocol/StarTram contract surfaces as compatibility-governed and CI-gated, but the identifiers themselves are mutable by in-package code.
Mutable canonical IDs increase risk of accidental reassignment during future refactors and blur stable contract boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Refactor contract identity exports to immutable-style APIs: keep raw IDs unexported, expose typed getter functions (`func UploadActionOpenEndpointID() ContractID`), and add a package test that verifies registry descriptors use those accessors and remain stable. Stage order: 1) introduce new accessors in `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`, 2) update call sites to use accessors, 3) add regression tests for identity stability, 4) run `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/actions ./startram`.

findings.summary: Contract governance is documented as manual prose instead of generated from runtime catalog.
evidence: README embeds detailed per-file governance requirements and CI gate scope as static prose.
Contract family declarations in `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` do not carry governance attributes that can be consumed to auto-produce policy docs/check inputs.
As contract families evolve, policy synchronization requires manual multi-location updates, increasing architectural drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Create a single machine-readable governance manifest in `goseg/protocol/contracts` (for families, required tests, lifecycle policy), then generate README governance snippets and gate inputs from it. Staged reorg plan: 1) add `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance_manifest.go` (or JSON under `goseg/protocol/contracts/metadata/`), 2) move family policy tokens from README prose into manifest-backed docs section, 3) update gate script/workflow consumers to read manifest, 4) validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts` and run existing gate script (`.github/scripts/check-upload-contract.sh`) in CI.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi API naming mixes `WiFi` and `Wifi`, reducing discoverability and grepability.
evidence: Service type is `WiFiRuntimeService` while public methods include `IsWifiEnabled`, `ListWifiSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi` in wifi_runtime_api.go.
Runtime internals also use mixed forms (`newWiFiRuntime`, `parseNmcliWifiDevices`, `ErrWifiInterfaceNotFound`) in wifi_runtime_methods.go.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one acronym convention (prefer `WiFi` consistently) and rename exported and internal symbols together in one refactor pass, with compatibility aliases only if external callers depend on old names.

findings.summary: README typed-contract guidance references `ActionToken`, but code contracts expose `ActionVerb`.
evidence: README lists typed APIs as `ContractID`, `ActionNamespace`, `ActionToken`, `ActionContractBinding` (README.md:99).
Contracts define action token type as `ActionVerb` (contracts.go:33-34), and protocol contract entries use `ActionVerb` constants (protocol_contracts.go:9-11).
findings.suggestion: Align governance text with runtime types by replacing `ActionToken` with `ActionVerb` in README, or introduce a real `ActionToken` alias/type and migrate APIs consistently.

findings.summary: Runtime context is initialized at import time and then mutated again during startup bootstrap.
evidence: Package init sets runtime context immediately (`init` + `setRuntimeContext(defaultRuntimeContext())`) in config.go.
Startup path calls `setDebugMode` and `initializePaths` to mutate context later in bootstrap.go, and main runtime snapshots consume context (`config.RuntimeContextSnapshot()`) in main.go.
findings.suggestion: Move runtime-context initialization to a single explicit bootstrap phase and keep package init side-effect free; make readers fail fast until bootstrap finalizes context.

findings.summary: Acronym casing conventions drift (`C2C` vs `C2c`, `WiFi` vs `Wifi`) across sibling APIs.
evidence: Protocol contracts use `C2CConnectAction`, while startup/main runtime hooks use `RunC2cCheckFn`, `StartC2cCheck`, `C2cCheckWith`.
Wi-Fi module mixes `WiFiRuntimeService` with `IsWifiEnabled`/`ConnectToWifi` method names.
findings.suggestion: Adopt and document a single acronym rule (for example, keep common initialisms fully capitalized) and apply it across C2C and Wi-Fi surfaces in one rename sweep.

findings.summary: Public long-running APIs inconsistently treat nil context as valid by silently using background context.
evidence: Subsystem and routine entrypoints repeatedly do `if ctx == nil { ctx = context.Background() }`, including event listeners and health loops.
Other boundary APIs (broadcast transitions) explicitly error on missing runtime dependency instead of silently defaulting, so caller expectations differ by module.
findings.suggestion: Define one boundary contract for lifecycle inputs (either reject nil context everywhere or consistently expose explicit `Background` wrappers) and refactor entrypoints to that contract.

findings.summary: Upload auth checks differ between session-open and upload-request paths due to nil request context.
evidence: OpenUploadEndpoint authorizes with `ValidateUploadSessionTokenFn(expectedToken, token, nil)` (importer.go:340).
validateUploadRequest authorizes with full request object and token headers (importer.go:645), creating different auth input surface for the same upload session lifecycle.
Open endpoint is an explicit external contract action (`ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`) in protocol_contracts.go.
findings.suggestion: Unify upload authorization semantics by requiring request-bound validation data in both steps (or formally split preflight vs request auth contracts and enforce both explicitly).
